summary: Adversarial design review of 1.10.0-RC3 multi-device presence before spec: 4 lenses + skeptic verify + synthesis
logs: Confirmed design findings: 10; clean lenses: none

survived.title: Crash of last device emits NO OFFLINE transition event — presence stuck ONLINE/AWAY forever (until reap, which is silent)
survived.scenario: The OFFLINE->ONLINE event correctness hinges entirely on the setPresence/clearPresence Lua running on the single hash. But a hard crash (kill -9, OOM, network partition of a node) NEVER calls clearPresence — onSessionRemoved (ClusterSessionHookImpl L170) only runs on graceful unbind. The dead device lingers in netty:presence:{userId} until reconciliation reaps it. The reap path is the design's removeAllForNode, which the design itself describes as 'SCAN netty:presence:*, HDEL nodeId-prefixed fields' — a bare HDEL. A bare HDEL does NOT run the atomic read-old/recompute-new/RETURN(old,new) Lua, so it produces NO (old,new) tuple and NO PRESENCE_CHANGE publish. Concretely: user U has exactly one device on node A (aggregate ONLINE). Node A is killed. clearPresence never runs. Reconciliation eventually HDELs U's field on the SCAN sweep — silently. Watchers were told U is ONLINE; they are NEVER told U went OFFLINE. The aggregate is now correctly OFFLINE in storage but the transition event the whole feature exists to deliver was dropped. This is the single most common real-world presence transition (a user closing a laptop / losing wifi rarely sends a graceful WebSocket close), and the design's core mechanism structurally cannot emit it. This is the RC1 shard-collapse-grade flaw: the headline guarantee fails on the dominant path.
survived.fix: removeAllForNode MUST be transition-aware, not a bare HDEL. For each presence hash it touches, run a Lua that: reads old aggregate from HVALS, HDELs all fields whose key starts with 'nodeId|', recomputes new aggregate, and RETURNs (userId, old, new) for every hash where old!=new. The reaper node then publishes one PRESENCE_CHANGE per changed user on __netty_presence__. Because reconciliation already does leader-election (ClusterNodeManager L499 reaper.tryClaim), exactly ONE node reaps a given dead node, so exactly one OFFLINE event per affected user is emitted — no dup. Also gate the design's claim 'each RC3 node invokes the listener' against this: the reaper must publish so the event reaches all nodes, since the reaping node is not necessarily a watcher's node.
survived.why:
SURVIVES (not refuted). Verified against grounding code; every load-bearing claim holds.

1) onSessionRemoved only fires on graceful unbind: CONFIRMED. ClusterSessionHookImpl.onSessionRemoved (L170) is the sole caller of userRegistry.unbindUser (L185). A kill -9 / OOM / partition never reaches it — the dead node's bindings persist until reconciliation. RC3's clearPresence (the transition-aware, (old,new)-emitting Lua) would live on this same graceful path.

2) The dead-node reap path is event-free: CONFIRMED in the analogous existing code. RedisUserRegistry.removeAllForNode (L154-199) is a pure SCAN + bare SREM — no read-old/recompute-new, no return tuple, no publish. RedisRoomRegistry.removeAllForNode is the same shape. There is NO PRESENCE_CHANGE / publish anywhere in the dead-node cleanup path today, and the UserRegistry SPI javadoc ("Reconciled via removeAllForNode on dead-node cleanup ... RC3 multi-device presence extends this same SPI", UserRegistry.java L28-31) plus release-notes-1.10.0.md confirm RC3 builds directly on this event-free reap. So RC3, as it would naturally be built, sources OFFLINE transition events only from the graceful clearPresence Lua and reaps crashed devices with a primitive that emits nothing.

3) The crash path is the DOMINANT real-world OFFLINE transition (laptop sleep / wifi loss rarely send a graceful close), so the single event the feature exists to deliver is exactly the one structurally dropped. This is the RC1 shard-collapse class: the headline guarantee fails on the majority path, not an edge case.

4) The proposed fix is concrete and correct against real code: ClusterNodeManager.doReconciliation (L499) already gates cleanup behind reaper.tryClaim leader-election, so exactly one node reaps a given dead node — a transition-aware reap that publishes one PRESENCE_CHANGE per affected user emits exactly once, no dup. The finding's secondary point (the reaping node is not necessarily a watcher's node, so the reap must PUBLISH to __netty_presence__ rather than only invoke a local listener) is also a genuine, grounded correctness nuance, since cleanup is centralized on one elected node.

Refutation attempts all fail: nothing in the SPI contract, javadoc, release notes, or cluster-design says the reap path emits a transition (it provably does not); the feature is RC3's in-development headline (dev-plan line 138, task #182), so it is in-scope, not hypothetical; and "storage eventually converges" does not save it because the EVENT — the entire deliverable of a presence subsystem — is lost while watchers remain told ONLINE forever.

Severity: the most common presence transition is structurally undeliverable to watchers — a lossy core-mechanism gap on the dominant path. BLOCKER is correctly calibrated; retained.

survived.title: PresenceRegistry.removeAllForNode is not actually wired into any dead-node fan-out — RC2's UserRegistry reap is already missing from invalidateCacheForNode
survived.scenario: The design asserts 'PresenceRegistry.removeAllForNode joins the reconciliation fan-out.' But the current fan-out does not include the RC2 UserRegistry at all, which is the parallel SPI the design says it mirrors. ClusterMessageSender.invalidateCacheForNode (L1050-1072 — the deadNodeCallback wired at L279) reaps ONLY roomRegistry.removeAllForNode (L1069); there is no userRegistry.removeAllForNode call anywhere (confirmed: the only removeAllForNode call sites are sessionRegistry in ClusterNodeManager L509 and roomRegistry in L1069). So RC2's user presence index already leaks dead-node sessions indefinitely on crash, and the RC3 design inherits a fan-out that has no slot for it. 'Joins the reconciliation fan-out' is therefore not a no-op extension of an existing pattern — it requires NEW wiring, and that wiring is on the deadNodeCallback path (invalidateCacheForNode), which runs AFTER sessionRegistry.removeAllForNode completes and is fire-and-forget with swallowed exceptions (L1069-1070 catches and only log.debug). If presence reaping fails there, the OFFLINE event from finding #1 is silently lost with only a debug log.
survived.fix: Decide explicitly where presence reaping hooks: either (a) extend invalidateCacheForNode to also call presenceRegistry.removeAllForNode AND publish the resulting transition events, or (b) add presence to the primary chained cleanup in ClusterNodeManager.doReconciliation alongside sessionRegistry.removeAllForNode (which is leader-elected and retried-on-failure via the .exceptionally re-queue at L528-532) rather than the best-effort callback. Option (b) is safer because the callback path swallows failures. The spec must also fix the latent RC2 gap (UserRegistry never reaped) or document why presence and the RC2 user-index diverge in reap wiring. Either way, 'joins the reconciliation fan-out' must name the exact call site and its failure/retry semantics.
survived.why:
VERIFIED as a real design flaw — survives. Grounding confirms every load-bearing claim:

(1) userRegistry.removeAllForNode is NEVER called in production. Across src/main the only removeAllForNode invocations are ClusterNodeManager.java:509 (sessionRegistry) and ClusterMessageSender.java:1069 (roomRegistry, in invalidateCacheForNode = the deadNodeCallback wired at ClusterMessageSender.java:279). RedisUserRegistry.removeAllForNode (RedisUserRegistry.java:154) is fully implemented but is dead code from the fan-out's perspective; only tests call it.

(2) The RC2 design TEXT explicitly claims this is handled and it is not. UserRegistry.java:28 javadoc: 'Reconciled via removeAllForNode on dead-node cleanup (parallel to SessionRegistry and ClusterRoomRegistry).' RC2 spec line 69 repeats it. False-as-written: no wiring exists. The auto-config (NettyWebSocketClusterConfigure.java:748-750) wires setUserRegistry but never adds it to the dead-node callback; the comment at L740-742 documents the hook calling ONLY the room registry's removeAllForNode.

(3) The leak is real/unbounded. ClusterSessionHookImpl.java:115 calls bindUser on connect (writes nodeId|b64uri|sessionId into Redis Set netty:user:{userId}); only clean disconnect calls unbindUser (L185). On node CRASH the dead node's members survive — Redis Sets carry no per-member TTL — so isUserOnline/sessionsForUser report a crashed node's sessions as online forever. This is exactly the false-online silent-loss window the sessionsForUser javadoc (L65-69, no-cache decision) was designed to prevent: sendToUser to a dead session → fire-and-forget unicast → no exception → no offline-queue fallback → silent message loss.

(4) RC3 inherits a fan-out with no presence slot, so 'PresenceRegistry.removeAllForNode joins the reconciliation fan-out' is NOT a no-op extension of an existing pattern — it requires NEW wiring the design does not specify.

(5) The failure-semantics asymmetry is real: room/user reap runs on the deadNodeCallback path (invalidateCacheForNode L1050-1072), fire-and-forget with swallowed exceptions (L1069-1070 catch→log.debug), and the callback itself is wrapped in try/catch→log.debug at ClusterNodeManager.java:523-525. By contrast sessionRegistry.removeAllForNode runs on the primary chained path with leader-election + .exceptionally retry-on-next-sweep (L528-533). A presence-OFFLINE reap hung on the callback path would be lost with only a debug log and no retry.

This is not hypothetical, not already-handled, not out-of-scope. The design document asserts a reconciliation behavior the code does not implement, and the RC3 design layers a presence transition onto that nonexistent hook. The fix correctly demands the spec name the exact call site and its failure/retry semantics, and decide whether RC3 also fixes the latent RC2 gap. Severity BLOCKER is justified: the RC3 design's load-bearing assertion is false-as-stated and the corruption/silent-loss it inherits is a correctness/safety issue, not cosmetic.

survived.title: Stale-ONLINE window after crash is unbounded by the design as written (bounded only by reconciliation interval); acceptable but must be quantified and documented, and getPresence must not over-promise
survived.scenario: Question (4): a node crashes; its devices linger ONLINE in the hash until reconciliation reaps them. The reconciliation interval default is 15000ms (ClusterNodeManager constructor doc L104) and heartbeat timeout default 10000ms, so the worst-case stale-ONLINE window is heartbeatTimeout + up-to-reconciliationInterval + leader-claim + SCAN time — roughly 25s+ in defaults, longer under SCAN load across many users. During this window getPresence(userId) returns aggregate=ONLINE and lists a dead device. This is the SAME staleness RC2 accepted for isUserOnline, but RC2 mitigated false-online silent-loss by making sendToUser do a fresh lookup AND fall back to the offline queue on send failure (ClusterMessageSender L838-858). getPresence has NO such fallback — it is a pure read with no delivery attempt, so a consumer that gates UI/business logic on getPresence().aggregate==ONLINE will act on a dead device for up to the full window with no self-correction.
survived.fix: Document the bound explicitly: stale-ONLINE window <= heartbeatTimeoutMs + reconciliationIntervalMs + SCAN time, and that getPresence reflects last-known state, NOT a liveness probe. Recommend in the javadoc/spec that latency-sensitive consumers treat getPresence as advisory and rely on the delivery-time fallback (sendToUser) for correctness, exactly as RC2 framed the no-cache decision. Consider adding the reap-emitted OFFLINE event (finding #1) as the authoritative correction signal so watchers self-heal at reap time.
survived.why:
SURVIVES as MINOR. Grounding confirms every load-bearing technical claim:

1. Defaults: ClusterNodeManager constructor doc (L103-104) confirms heartbeatTimeoutMs=10000, reconciliationIntervalMs=15000. The ~25s worst-case stale-ONLINE window math is sound.

2. Reap is interval-bounded with NO eager presence-OFFLINE event: doReconciliation() (L492) runs scheduleAtFixedRate at reconciliationIntervalMs (L205-206). On reap it calls sessionRegistry.removeAllForNode + the route-cache deadNodeCallback (L509-526) but emits no presence event. A crashed node's userId→session bindings linger until the next sweep reaps them via UserRegistry.removeAllForNode (UserRegistry L85-90, which RC3 presence reuses per L29-31). So the stale-ONLINE window is genuinely bounded only by heartbeatTimeout + reconciliationInterval + leader-claim + SCAN.

3. The sendToUser self-correction the finding contrasts against is real: ClusterMessageSender L816-858 does a FRESH uncached lookup and, when no bound session is reachable at send time, falls back to the offline queue (L853-858). A pure-read presence API cannot replicate this self-healing — the finding's core contrast is accurate.

4. The existing UserRegistry no-cache javadoc (L62-74) documents staleness from CACHING only; it does NOT cover reconciliation-bounded staleness after a crash (an uncached read still returns the dead node's lingering bindings until reap). So the finding is not already-handled by the design as stated.

Skeptic stress points that nearly refute it: (a) There is no getPresence/PresenceRegistry API in code or any RC3 spec yet — RC3 is in_progress with no written design. The reviewer is anticipating the API. (b) Severity is self-conceded MINOR and the fix is documentation/advisory-framing, not a delivery-correctness bug; the finding explicitly concedes delivery stays correct via the sendToUser fallback.

Why it still survives: development-plan.md L138 commits RC3 to a PresenceRegistry with online/away/offline per device, and an aggregate presence READ is the irreducible core of the feature — so the finding is not a strawman, it names the one new read surface RC3 must add. The gap is real ambiguity-by-omission: if RC3 ships an aggregate-ONLINE read without documenting that it is last-known-state bounded by heartbeatTimeout+reconciliationInterval and is NOT a liveness probe, a consumer gating UI/business logic on getPresence().aggregate==ONLINE acts on a dead device for ~25s with zero self-correction — precisely the contract RC2 deliberately spelled out for sessionsForUser but which a new pure read does not inherit. For a DESIGN review of the about-to-be-written RC3, flagging this contract requirement is in-scope and concrete. MINOR is the correct severity: no loss/unsafety, but a genuine design-contract gap the reviewer should fix before the API is frozen.

survived.title: Reserving __netty_presence__ as a broadcast 'topic' silently maps it into the netty:broadcast:* keyspace and the SAME EnvelopeCodec/onBroadcastMessage path used by real URIs — collision and self-suppression must be designed in
survived.scenario: The design says presence rides 'a RESERVED topic __netty_presence__' via the existing broker. But ClusterBroker has no notion of a separate topic namespace — RedisPubSubBroker.publish(uri, env) (line 206) and subscribe(uri,...) (line 235) both compute `channel = BROADCAST_PREFIX + uri` = `netty:broadcast:__netty_presence__`. That means: (1) the presence channel lives in the EXACT same Redis channel keyspace as a real @MessageMapping URI — if an application ever registers a WebSocket mapping literally named `__netty_presence__`, its broadcasts and presence events collide on one channel and cross-decode (a real BROADCAST data message would be fanned out to the PresenceChangeListener and vice-versa). (2) If presence is delivered via broker.subscribe()/onBroadcastMessage, note onBroadcastMessage (lines 578-594) is hard-wired to `deserializePayload()` + `localSender.topicMessage(uri,...)` — it would try to deliver a presence envelope as an application message to local sessions on URI `__netty_presence__`. So presence CANNOT reuse onBroadcastMessage; it needs its OWN listener registered via broker.subscribe("__netty_presence__", this::onPresenceMessage). The design does not name this listener or its registration site, and ClusterMessageSender.start() only subscribes broadcast channels for `localSender.getRegisteredUri()` (lines 309-311) — `__netty_presence__` is not a registered URI, so the presence subscription needs an explicit, unconditional subscribe at start() gated on presence.enable.
survived.fix: In the spec: (1) Reserve the name explicitly and forbid it as an app URI — validate at mapping-registration that no @MessageMapping equals `__netty_presence__` (fail fast at startup, like the existing pipe-in-URI guard). Prefer a name that cannot collide with a real URI path, e.g. a control prefix the URI validator already rejects. (2) Specify a DEDICATED listener `onPresenceMessage(envelope)` subscribed via `broker.subscribe("__netty_presence__", this::onPresenceMessage)` from ClusterMessageSender.start() (or the presence component's start), gated on presence.enable, NOT routed through onBroadcastMessage. (3) State that this subscription is unconditional (not driven by getRegisteredUri) so a node with zero local sessions still receives presence events for users it watches.
survived.why:
SURVIVES as MAJOR. I verified every load-bearing code claim against the grounding sources:

1. No topic namespace exists. RedisPubSubBroker.publish(uri, env) (line 206) and subscribe(uri, listener) (line 235) BOTH compute `channel = BROADCAST_PREFIX + uri` = `netty:broadcast:` + uri. A "reserved topic __netty_presence__" routed "via the existing broker" (the design's own words) genuinely lands in the SAME Redis channel keyspace as a real @MessageMapping URI. Collision with an app-registered `__netty_presence__` URI is therefore mechanically possible.

2. onBroadcastMessage (ClusterMessageSender lines 578-594) is hard-wired to deserializePayload() + localSender.topicMessage(envelope.getUri(), message). subscribeBroadcast (line 976) registers exactly `this::onBroadcastMessage` as the listener for every broadcast channel. So if presence reuses broker.subscribe(), a presence envelope WOULD be decoded as an AbstractMessage and fanned out to local sessions on the URI — presence genuinely cannot reuse this path and needs its own listener (e.g. onPresenceMessage) or its own MessageKind, as the finding states.

3. start() (lines 309-311) only subscribes broadcast channels for localSender.getRegisteredUri(). A reserved topic name is not a registered URI, so the presence subscription must be explicit and unconditional (gated on presence.enable, not driven by getRegisteredUri) — otherwise a node with no local sessions on that URI never receives presence events for users it watches. Verified.

Skeptic tests applied and the finding still holds:
- Not a strawman: the finding evaluates the design exactly as described ("reserved topic via the existing broker") and shows the broker's broadcast-topic mechanism is the wrong, lossy vehicle. The design-as-stated points at the broadcast path whose sole registered listener is onBroadcastMessage.
- Not already-handled: there is no PRESENCE MessageKind (ClusterEnvelope.MessageKind has only BROADCAST/UNICAST/CLOSE/ROOM_BROADCAST), no onPresenceMessage, and no committed RC3 spec on disk (the token __netty_presence__ appears nowhere in the repo; dev-plan lists RC3 as "规划/planning" only). So the listener/registration/suppression decisions are genuinely unspecified, not implied-and-obvious.
- The established RC1 precedent (ROOM_BROADCAST rides the per-node UNICAST channel with its own MessageKind, dispatched by kind in onUnicastMessage) proves the team makes exactly these routing/dispatch decisions at design time and documents them — reinforcing that "reserved topic via the existing broker" is an under-specified, wrong-mechanism design choice the review legitimately flags.

Severity correctly MAJOR, not BLOCKER: this is under-specification / wrong-mechanism in an in-design RC caught before implementation, not data loss in a shipped system. The collision-with-real-URI leg is the weakest (low real-world probability, trivially reserved), but the core (mis-dispatch via onBroadcastMessage + missing dedicated listener + missing unconditional subscription) is concrete and correct. Note one minor inaccuracy in the FIX text — it references "the existing pipe-in-URI guard" as enforcement, but the pipe limitation in SimpleTextEnvelopeCodec is documentation-only (a Javadoc note), not an enforced startup guard; this does not weaken the finding (if anything it strengthens the need for explicit name-reservation validation).

survived.title: Self-delivery suppression: the publishing node receives its own PRESENCE_CHANGE echo on the reserved channel and will double-fire its local PresenceChangeListener unless originNodeId suppression is explicitly applied
survived.scenario: Redis Pub/Sub always delivers a publish back to the publisher's own subscription. Every existing receive path guards this with originNodeId: onBroadcastMessage line 580 (`if (nodeManager.getNodeId().equals(envelope.getOriginNodeId())) { selfDeliveryDropped++; return; }`), onReliableMessage line 1017, onRoomMessage line 721. The presence design publishes PRESENCE_CHANGE on `__netty_presence__` AND the same node subscribes to it (so it can react to OTHER nodes' presence changes for users it watches). Without an explicit originNodeId self-suppression in onPresenceMessage, the origin node will invoke its app-supplied PresenceChangeListener(userId, old, new) TWICE for its own transition: once locally at the setPresence/clearPresence hook site, and once again when its own echo arrives. The app then double-pushes the presence update to watchers (duplicate notifications) and the metrics events_received double-counts. Worse, the design's whole correctness story is 'exactly ONE OFFLINE->ONLINE event' from the Lua transition detection — a self-echo silently violates that at the listener level even though the Lua did its job.
survived.fix: Specify that onPresenceMessage MUST drop the envelope when `envelope.getOriginNodeId().equals(localNodeId)` (mirror onBroadcastMessage line 580), counting it as a presence self-delivery drop meter. ALSO decide and document where the LOCAL listener fires: either (a) the transition hook fires the local PresenceChangeListener directly and publishes for OTHER nodes only (origin suppresses its own echo) — this is the existing local-first/cluster-second pattern and the right choice; or (b) the listener fires ONLY on receive and the origin does NOT self-suppress (no local-first fire) — simpler but adds a Redis round-trip of latency to the local listener and breaks if the broker is degraded. Pick (a) to match topicMessage/roomMessage semantics, and state it explicitly so the impl doesn't fire in both places.
survived.why:
The finding survives but is over-rated; corrected severity is MINOR.

WHY IT SURVIVES (not refuted): The premise is sound, not a strawman. Cluster-wide presence fan-out over Pub/Sub forces the exact topology the finding describes — a node both publishes PRESENCE_CHANGE and subscribes to learn OTHER nodes' transitions — which mirrors topicMessage/onBroadcastMessage one-for-one. Redis Pub/Sub provably echoes a publish back to the publisher's own subscription. The harm is concrete and demonstrable: if onPresenceMessage omits an originNodeId guard AND the local listener also fires at the bind/transition hook site, the origin node invokes the app-supplied PresenceChangeListener TWICE for its own transition → duplicate watcher notifications + double-counted events_received, violating the 'exactly ONE OFFLINE->ONLINE event' story at the listener level even though the Lua transition detection produced a single transition. That is a precise lossy/incorrect outcome, not a vague observation. The genuinely load-bearing, non-obvious part of the finding is its fix point (ii): the design must DECIDE and STATE *where* the local listener fires (hook-site-then-suppress-echo vs receive-only-no-suppress). The presence case has an extra app-facing callback (PresenceChangeListener) distinct from data delivery, so this fork is real and is NOT mechanically resolved by the existing self-suppression convention — the convention covers the echo guard but says nothing about listener fire-site. This is a legitimate undetermined design decision worth pinning in the spec.

GROUNDING: Confirmed the universal self-suppression convention in ClusterMessageSender — onBroadcastMessage (L580), onRoomMessage (L721), onReliableMessage (L1017) all open with the identical `if (nodeManager.getNodeId().equals(envelope.getOriginNodeId())) return;` guard; ClusterEnvelope (L62, L121) documents originNodeId as existing specifically for self-suppression; the class-level 'local-first, cluster-second' principle (L39-50) is the documented design law. The RC2 ClusterSessionHookImpl (bindUser L115 / unbindUser L185) is where presence transitions originate, so RC3 would layer Lua transition detection there. The RC3 presence design is not yet a written spec (only a one-line roadmap entry in development-plan.md L138) — so the design 'as described' is genuinely unwritten on this exact decision, which is why a design-review clarification is in-scope.

WHY MINOR, NOT MAJOR: The suppression mechanism (originNodeId in every envelope) already exists and is applied with ironclad uniformity across all 3 existing receive callbacks; the RC1 room design review (2026-06-08) already treated 'self-suppression' as a baked-in design INTENT, not a gap. Any implementer following the entrenched pattern would near-certainly apply the onPresenceMessage guard — so the finding's framing that the origin 'WILL double-fire unless explicitly applied' overstates likelihood. Worst case is duplicate notifications + a metric double-count, both recoverable (presence is state-convergent/idempotent at the app layer) with NO message/data loss, and the core single-transition guarantee from the Lua is intact. The real residual is a spec-must-state-this clarification about the listener fire-site — a documentation/design-precision gap, not a design that is actively wrong or lossy as conceived. That is MINOR.

survived.title: OnAnyRedisSpiRequired does not consider presence.enable — presence-only deployment with a fully-custom SPI set gets its Redis client/connection gated OFF, so the default RedisPresenceRegistry has no connection to inject
survived.scenario: OnAnyRedisSpiRequired (the @Conditional on nettyClusterRedisClient/nettyClusterRedisConnection, config L140/L248) currently returns true only when (a) one of the 4 core SPIs is still defaulting to Redis, OR (b) room.enable && no custom ClusterRoomRegistry, OR (c) offline.enable && (no custom UserRegistry || no custom OfflineQueueStore) — see OnAnyRedisSpiRequired L89-92: it reads ONLY 'server.netty.websocket.cluster.offline.enable'. RC3's RedisPresenceRegistry needs nettyClusterRedisConnection (the design says 'co-located with netty:user:{b64userId}', single standalone Redis). Concrete failure: a user runs presence.enable=true, offline.enable=false, and supplies their own ClusterBroker+SessionRegistry+ClusterNodeHeartbeat+ClusterReaper beans (the documented all-custom-SPI path this condition exists to optimize). All 4 hasBean() checks pass, roomEnabled=false, offlineEnabled=false → matches() returns false → NO RedisClient and NO nettyClusterRedisConnection bean is created. The presence.enable-gated RedisPresenceRegistry bean then fails to autowire @Qualifier("nettyClusterRedisConnection") → NoSuchBeanDefinitionException at context startup. This is the exact class of bug OnAnyRedisSpiRequired was added (V1.9.0-RC16) to prevent, and RC2 already had to extend it for offline (L86-92).
survived.fix: Add a presence clause to OnAnyRedisSpiRequired.matches(): boolean presenceEnabled = Boolean.parseBoolean(env.getProperty("...cluster.presence.enable","false")); return ... || (presenceEnabled && !hasBean(bf, PresenceRegistry.class)). Add a context test (presence-only + all-4-custom-SPI) asserting the Redis connection bean exists — mirror the RC2 offline test.
survived.why:
CONFIRMED — concrete design-wiring gap, not hypothetical. Verified against the live code.

OnAnyRedisSpiRequired.matches() (netty-spring-websocket-cluster/.../support/OnAnyRedisSpiRequired.java L70-93) inspects exactly: the 4 core SPIs (SessionRegistry/ClusterBroker/ClusterNodeHeartbeat/ClusterReaper, L72-77), then room.enable (L81-82) and offline.enable (L89-90 — it literally reads only "server.netty.websocket.cluster.offline.enable"). There is no presence clause. This @Conditional gates both nettyClusterRedisClient (NettyWebSocketClusterConfigure.java L140) and nettyClusterRedisConnection (L248): when it returns false, NEITHER the RedisClient nor the @Qualifier("nettyClusterRedisConnection") connection bean is created.

The established invariant pattern in this file: every standalone Redis-backed gated SPI (RedisRoomRegistry L508-517, RedisUserRegistry L542-551, RedisOfflineQueueStore L554-568, RedisSessionRegistry L493-500, RedisClusterNodeHeartbeat, RedisClusterReaper) carries STANDALONE_REDIS_REGISTRY + @ConditionalOnProperty(...enable=true) + injects @Qualifier("nettyClusterRedisConnection"). RC1 (room) and RC2 (offline) EACH had to add their own clause to OnAnyRedisSpiRequired precisely so the all-custom-SPI deployment doesn't gate the connection off (room clause L83-84 added RC1; offline clause L86-92 added RC2). The docs confirm RC3's PresenceRegistry follows this same mold — development-plan.md L138 ("多设备 presence（PresenceRegistry…扩展 RC2 的 UserRegistry）") and the RC2 spec (2026-06-14-offline-queue-rc2.md L26/L69-70/L254) repeatedly state RC3 extends the same Redis-backed UserRegistry SPI on the standalone Redis path.

Concrete failure (matches the finding exactly): presence.enable=true, offline.enable=false, all 4 core SPIs user-supplied (the AllFourSpiOverridesConfig path — a tested, supported deployment, see test L600-619 clusterMode_allFourSpiOverridden_doesNotCreateRedisClient). All 4 hasBean checks pass → roomEnabled=false → offlineEnabled=false → matches() returns false → no RedisClient, no nettyClusterRedisConnection. With offline off, no RedisUserRegistry is created either, so there is no shared connection to piggyback on. The presence.enable-gated default RedisPresenceRegistry then fails to autowire @Qualifier("nettyClusterRedisConnection") → NoSuchBeanDefinitionException/UnsatisfiedDependencyException at context startup, hard, no degraded mode.

Refutation attempts all fail: (a) "presence reuses UserRegistry's connection" — no, presence is gated independently on presence.enable, and with offline.enable=false the UserRegistry bean and its connection both don't exist; (b) "maybe RC3 won't be Redis-standalone-gated" — that would be an unjustified break from the project's own consistent SPI-wiring convention; the design as documented is Redis-standalone. The finding names the exact condition (L89-92 reads only offline.enable), the exact failure, and a fix that mirrors two existing precedents plus an existing test to mirror (the RC2 offline + AllFourSpi context test). This is a real omission in a design-level component whose entire contract is "match when any default Redis SPI needs the connection."

survived.title: PresenceRegistry must NOT join the userRegistry dead-node fan-out as designed — that fan-out path does not actually clean UserRegistry today; it is wired only for roomRegistry, so 'join the reconciliation fan-out' targets a hook that half-cleans the parallel SPI
survived.scenario: The design states: 'Dead-node reaping: PresenceRegistry.removeAllForNode joins the reconciliation fan-out (SCAN netty:presence:*, HDEL nodeId-prefixed fields).' I traced the actual fan-out. ClusterNodeManager.doReconciliation (L509) calls ONLY sessionRegistry.removeAllForNode(dead), then on completion invokes deadNodeCallback (L521-525), which is ClusterMessageSender.invalidateCacheForNode (wired at L279). invalidateCacheForNode (L1050-1072) cleans: nodeCache, reliableBroker consumer groups, and — only when roomRegistry!=null — roomNodeSetCache + roomRegistry.removeAllForNode. It NEVER calls userRegistry.removeAllForNode. Confirmed by grep: across the whole main source tree the ONLY call site of *.removeAllForNode for a derived index is rr.removeAllForNode at L1069; userRegistry.removeAllForNode (declared in UserRegistry.java L90 and implemented in RedisUserRegistry L154, with the SPI javadoc claiming 'Reconciled via removeAllForNode on dead-node cleanup') is dead code — never invoked. So when a node crashes, its stale entries in netty:user:{b64userId} are NEVER reaped (only its session-registry rows are). RC3 inheriting 'join the fan-out' means: if presence wiring copies the (non-existent) user wiring, presence is never reaped either → a crashed node's devices stay forever ONLINE in every netty:presence hash → the user AGGREGATE is permanently wrong (false-ONLINE), and no PRESENCE_CHANGE→OFFLINE event ever fires. The aggregate-correctness mechanism (the whole point of RC3) is defeated by a missing reconciliation edge.
survived.fix: Before RC3, fix the fan-out to actually clean derived indexes: in invalidateCacheForNode add userRegistry.removeAllForNode(nodeId) when userRegistry!=null AND presenceRegistry.removeAllForNode(nodeId) when presenceRegistry!=null (parallel to the existing roomRegistry block). Additionally, recompute+publish a PRESENCE_CHANGE on the leader node after removeAllForNode for any user whose aggregate flipped — the Lua transition-detection only fires on setPresence/clearPresence, not on bulk node-reap, so reaping alone produces a state change with NO event unless the reaper explicitly diffs and publishes. Spec this explicitly; it is the multi-node analogue of RC2's drain-lock must-fix.
survived.why:
VERIFIED — the finding is a real design flaw, not refuted. Grounding confirms every load-bearing claim:

1. ClusterNodeManager.doReconciliation (L492-543) on dead-node detection calls ONLY sessionRegistry.removeAllForNode(dead), then on completion fires deadNodeCallback (L521-525).
2. deadNodeCallback is wired to ClusterMessageSender.invalidateCacheForNode (L279, this::invalidateCacheForNode).
3. invalidateCacheForNode (L1050-1072) cleans nodeCache, reliableBroker consumer groups, and — ONLY when this.roomRegistry != null (L1067) — roomNodeSetCache.clear() + rr.removeAllForNode(nodeId) (L1069). There is NO userRegistry branch anywhere in the method.
4. Grep across the entire main source tree: the only derived-index *.removeAllForNode call site is rr.removeAllForNode at ClusterMessageSender L1069. userRegistry.removeAllForNode is declared (UserRegistry.java L90), implemented (RedisUserRegistry L154), and its SPI javadoc explicitly claims 'Reconciled via removeAllForNode on dead-node cleanup (parallel to SessionRegistry and ClusterRoomRegistry)' (UserRegistry.java L28) — but it is never invoked. Confirmed dead code.
5. The sender's two userRegistry usages (L806 sendToUser, L885 isUserOnline) are both on the user-addressed delivery path, NOT the reconciliation path.
6. The starter (NettyWebSocketClusterConfigure L748-750) wires sender.setUserRegistry(userRegistry) but — unlike the roomRegistry block at L743-746 whose comment claims it 'also activates the dead-node hook' — setting userRegistry activates NO dead-node hook, because invalidateCacheForNode has no userRegistry branch to activate.

Consequence as the finding states: when a node crashes, its stale netty:user:{b64userId} bindings are never reaped (only session-registry rows are). RC2 isUserOnline/sessionsForUser are deliberately uncached (UserRegistry L65-69) and read the store directly, so a crashed node leaves its users permanently false-ONLINE in the user index.

Why this is a DESIGN flaw and survives skeptical refutation: the RC3 design text says 'PresenceRegistry.removeAllForNode joins the reconciliation fan-out.' That fan-out, as actually wired, does NOT clean the parallel UserRegistry SPI — so the instruction targets a hook that half-cleans (roomRegistry only). If RC3 presence wiring mirrors the (non-existent) user wiring, presence is never reaped → a crashed node's devices stay forever ONLINE in every netty:presence hash → the user AGGREGATE is permanently wrong and no PRESENCE_CHANGE→OFFLINE event ever fires. Aggregate correctness is the entire point of RC3, so the design-as-described defeats its own goal. This is concrete, not hypothetical, not already-handled (the method demonstrably lacks the branch), and in-scope for the identity-gating-wiring lens.

The finding's second design gap is also valid: even if removeAllForNode is wired, bulk node-reap does not run the per-event Lua transition-detection (which only fires on setPresence/clearPresence), so reaping flips aggregate state with NO PRESENCE_CHANGE event unless the reaper explicitly diffs+publishes — this must be spec'd. This is the multi-node analogue of RC2's drain-lock must-fix.

Severity: BLOCKER is correct. The defect both (a) reflects a pre-existing RC2 dead-code reap that makes the user index permanently wrong after any crash, and (b) makes the RC3 design-as-described non-functional for its core purpose. Either alone is release-blocking for the presence feature; together, leaving the fan-out edge missing ships an aggregate that silently lies after every node failure. The fix is precisely scoped: add userRegistry/presenceRegistry branches to invalidateCacheForNode parallel to the roomRegistry block, plus a leader-side recompute+publish of PRESENCE_CHANGE for any user whose aggregate flipped during reap.

survived.title: ClusterSessionHookImpl's single offlineEnabled flag + 7-arg constructor does not cleanly split into the three independent capabilities (identity / offline / presence) the refactor requires
survived.scenario: Today the hook collapses identity+offline into ONE boolean: ClusterSessionHookImpl L84 sets offlineEnabled = offlineEnabled && userIdResolver!=null && userRegistry!=null && offlineStore!=null, and L98/L175 gate the ENTIRE identity path (resolve userId, register WITH userId metadata, bindUser) on that single flag. RC3 needs three orthogonal capabilities: (A) identity = resolver+userRegistry present → resolve userId, write userId into register metadata, bindUser/unbindUser; (B) offline = (A) + offlineStore → drainOnConnect + delete-ack; (C) presence = (A) + presenceRegistry → setPresence(ONLINE) after bindUser / clearPresence on removal + publish-on-transition. With the current single flag, presence-only (offline off) yields offlineEnabled=false → onSessionRegistered takes the else branch (L122-128) → register(emptyMap), NEVER resolves userId, NEVER binds the user. So bindUser is never called, netty:user is never populated, and there is no userId to setPresence on. Presence cannot function. The constructor (L74-88) also has no presenceRegistry parameter and the config bean (NettyWebSocketClusterConfigure L778-797) computes a single offlineEnabled from offline.isEnable() only.
survived.fix: Refactor the hook into independent flags: identityEnabled = resolver!=null && userRegistry!=null; offlineEnabled = identityEnabled && offlineStore!=null; presenceEnabled = identityEnabled && presenceRegistry!=null. Gate the resolve+register-with-userId+bindUser block on identityEnabled (so presence-only still binds), the drainOnConnect block on offlineEnabled, and the setPresence/clearPresence+publish block on presenceEnabled. Add presenceRegistry to the constructor and update the config bean to compute all three and pass presenceRegistry. Add a context/unit test for the presence-only path asserting bindUser+setPresence fire and drain does not.
survived.why:
VERIFIED against source. The finding names a concrete, real gating defect that the RC3 presence design (which the SPI doc says reuses UserRegistry/bindUser as the presence substrate) must fix.

Load-bearing facts confirmed in ClusterSessionHookImpl.java:
- L84: this.offlineEnabled = offlineEnabled && userIdResolver != null && userRegistry != null && offlineStore != null — identity (resolver+userRegistry) and offline (offlineStore) are collapsed into ONE boolean.
- L98: userId = userIdResolver.resolve(session) runs only inside if (offlineEnabled).
- L106-128: the register-WITH-userId-metadata call (L110), bindUser (L115), and drainOnConnect (L116) all live inside the if (userId != null) block, reachable only when offlineEnabled is true. The else branch (L122-128) calls registryWriter.register(uri, sessionId, nodeId, Collections.emptyMap()) and never binds the user.
- Constructor (L74-88) has no presenceRegistry parameter.

Confirmed in NettyWebSocketClusterConfigure.java L792-793: the config bean computes a single offlineEnabled = properties.getOffline().isEnable() && userIdResolver!=null && userRegistry!=null && offlineQueueStore!=null, and passes only that.

Confirmed in RedisUserRegistry.java: bindUser (L88) is what populates netty:user:* (USER_PREFIX="netty:user:" L72) — i.e., bindUser IS the presence-index population. UserRegistry.java SPI doc L23/L31 explicitly calls UserRegistry "a derived routing/presence index" and states "RC3 multi-device presence extends this same SPI." development-plan.md L138 lists RC3 PresenceRegistry (per-device online/away/offline, extending RC2's UserRegistry) as planned ("规划"), with no PresenceRegistry SPI existing yet.

Consequence is exactly as the finding states: in a presence-only config (offline.enable=false, presence enabled), offlineStore is null → offlineEnabled=false → onSessionRegistered takes the else branch → register(emptyMap), never resolves userId, never calls bindUser, netty:user is never populated → there is no userId and no bound session for presence to setPresence(ONLINE) on. Presence-only cannot function.

Refutation attempts all fail: (1) Not already-handled — there is no identityEnabled/presenceEnabled split and the constructor/config bean have no presenceRegistry. (2) Not out-of-scope — presence-only is a legitimate named RC3 configuration and the RC3 design reuses bindUser/UserRegistry, so it inherits this gating. (3) Not hypothetical — the break is mechanically demonstrable from the current branch structure.

Severity MAJOR is correct, not BLOCKER: presence still works in the common IM config where offline is also enabled, and the fix is a clean flag-split refactor (no data loss/security hole). But presence-only silently doing nothing, plus the missing presenceRegistry constructor/config-bean parameter, is a genuine functional/wiring defect well above MINOR. The proposed fix (split into identityEnabled = resolver!=null && userRegistry!=null; offlineEnabled = identityEnabled && offlineStore!=null; presenceEnabled = identityEnabled && presenceRegistry!=null; gate resolve+register-with-userId+bindUser on identityEnabled, drain on offlineEnabled, setPresence/clearPresence on presenceEnabled; add presenceRegistry to constructor + config bean) is the correct remediation.

survived.title: getPresence exposing 'nodeId|sessionId' device keys leaks internal cluster topology and gives the app NO stable device identity to build a real multi-device UX
survived.scenario: PresenceOperations.getPresence(userId) returns UserPresence{aggregate, Map<device,status>} where device = 'nodeId|sessionId'. Two problems compound. (1) LEAK: nodeId is internal cluster routing topology (a UUID/host id the app should never see or persist); baking it into the public device-key contract couples the app to cluster internals and will break the moment RC4 mesh changes node identity semantics. (2) NO STABLE IDENTITY: sessionId is per-WS-session — it changes on every reconnect. So the 'device' map is really a 'live-session' map. An IM multi-device feature ('show me my phone vs my laptop', 'sign out my other device', 'this message was read on device X') needs a STABLE per-physical-device id that survives reconnects. Per-session granularity cannot express any of that: a user who reconnects looks like a brand-new device every time, and the same phone across two reconnects is two unrelated map entries. RC2 has no deviceId concept, so RC3 is inventing device identity — and it is inventing the WRONG one (ephemeral + topology-leaking) and freezing it into a public API that RC4/GA will be stuck supporting.
survived.fix: Do not key the public map on 'nodeId|sessionId'. Introduce a deviceId concept that the app supplies at handshake (parallel to how UserIdResolver supplies userId — add a DeviceIdResolver SPI, or carry deviceId in the same handshake metadata). getPresence then returns Map<deviceId,status> with NO nodeId exposed. If a per-physical-device id is out of scope for RC3, then DROP the device-level map from the public API entirely and return only the aggregate + a device COUNT — that is honest (it is genuinely per-session/ephemeral) and avoids freezing a leaky, unstable key into the contract. Either way, internal 'nodeId|sessionId' hash fields stay internal (they are fine as the Redis HASH field for reaping), but must not surface in the SPI return type. This is a one-way-door API decision; get it right before the spec.
survived.why:
SURVIVES. Grounding confirms the finding's premise and its two sub-claims, and it lands at exactly the right gate (no RC3 spec exists yet; this is a one-way-door public-API decision being reviewed pre-spec).

PREMISE IS WELL-GROUNDED, NOT A STRAWMAN. The finding asserts RC3's getPresence would return Map<device,status> keyed on 'nodeId|sessionId'. That is the natural extension of RC2's actual building blocks: RedisUserRegistry encodes each session member as `nodeId|b64uri|sessionId` (RedisUserRegistry.java:231-234) and returns the public SessionRef = (nodeId, uri, sessionId) (SessionRef.java; spi/UserRegistry.java#sessionsForUser). The UserRegistry javadoc explicitly says "RC3 multi-device presence extends this same SPI" (UserRegistry.java:31; echoed in cluster-design.md:513 and release-notes-1.10.0.md:256). So surfacing the existing member encoding as the device key is the obvious default design, not an invented one.

SUB-CLAIM 2 (NO STABLE IDENTITY) is the load-bearing, hard-to-refute half and holds. sessionId is explicitly ephemeral/per-connection (UserOperations.java:29 "ephemeral per-connection sessionId"); only userId carries a stability contract (UserIdResolver.java:26-27). A grep for deviceId/device-id/DeviceId across the repo returns nothing — RC2 has NO device-identity concept, so RC3 is genuinely inventing one, and the only available building block (nodeId|sessionId) is ephemeral (changes every reconnect) and topology-coupled. A per-live-session map cannot express the core multi-device IM UX the RC is named for ("show phone vs laptop", "sign out my other device", "read on device X"): the same phone across two reconnects appears as two unrelated entries.

SUB-CLAIM 1 (nodeId LEAK) is weaker but still valid: nodeId is internal routing topology, and RC4 is explicitly "node-to-node mesh / gossip membership, Redis off the hot path" (development-plan.md:139) — node-identity semantics are in flux. Caveat: nodeId already appears in the low-level SessionRef routing SPI, so "the app must never see nodeId" is not absolute today; but baking it into an app-facing IM-UX presence/device contract (vs a routing SPI) is the specific, defensible concern.

REFUTATION ATTEMPTS REJECTED: (a) "per-session is honest scope, like RC2 per-user" — but the design LABELS the key 'device' and freezes it into a public getPresence return type; an ephemeral session masquerading as a 'device' is a semantic mismatch in a one-way-door API, not a documented honest limitation. (b) "already handled" — false: no RC3 spec exists; the decision is unmade. (c) "hypothetical" — false: it names a concrete, wrong public-API contract with a concrete fix (DeviceIdResolver SPI parallel to UserIdResolver, or drop the device map and return aggregate + device COUNT).

This is a real scope-semantics design flaw caught at the correct gate.

survived.title: Per-session 'device' granularity makes the AWAY model and aggregate semantically noisy for the multi-tab / multi-connection reality it claims to model
survived.scenario: Aggregate is 'ONLINE if any device ONLINE; AWAY if >=1 device but none ONLINE.' With device == WS session (per the getPresence key), a single browser user with 3 tabs open = 3 'devices'. If the app sets one tab AWAY (e.g., tab loses focus) but the other two stay ONLINE, the aggregate is correctly ONLINE — fine. But the device MAP the app sees now shows 3 entries for what the user experiences as one device, two ONLINE one AWAY, which is meaningless to surface ('you are online on 3 devices' when it's one laptop). More sharply: setPresence is per-session, so an app trying to implement 'set me AWAY' has to call setPresence(AWAY) on the specific session it happens to be handling — it cannot say 'set THIS USER (all my sessions) AWAY' without iterating sessions it may not all see locally. The per-session model leaks the connection-multiplicity of WebSocket into a feature whose mental model is per-user/per-device.
survived.fix: Tie this to the device-identity fix above: if a stable deviceId is introduced, collapse multiple sessions of the same deviceId into one device status (last-writer-wins or any-ONLINE-wins per device), so 3 tabs of one laptop = one ONLINE device. If per-session is kept for RC3, document explicitly that 'device' means 'live connection' (rename it in the API to avoid the word 'device', e.g. Map<connectionId,status>) and provide a setPresenceForUser(userId, status) convenience that the app can call once instead of per-session — otherwise 'set me away' is awkward to implement on top of a per-session primitive.
survived.why:
The finding survives refutation as a real (MINOR) scope-semantics/API-ergonomics flaw in the proposed RC3 design. It is grounded in the actual data model, not hypothetical.

GROUNDING: RC3 (task #182) is in_progress with no spec yet, so the design under review is the natural design path inherited from RC2. RC2 ships UserRegistry mapping userId -> Set<SessionRef>, where SessionRef = (nodeId, uri, sessionId) (verified in spi/SessionRef.java and room/RedisUserRegistry.java: Redis Set members are `nodeId|b64uri|sessionId`, keyed/bound per sessionId). There is NO stable device identity anywhere in the codebase — a WS session is the only granularity available. The RC2 spec (line 245) and release-notes (lines 169-170, 313) already use the word "device" ("per-device offline backfill (RC3)", "multi-device presence builds on UserRegistry"), while the underlying index is purely session-keyed. So the finding's premise (device == session, and the word "device" is already in use) is factual.

CLAIM A (semantic noise / mislabeling "device") is real: a 3-tab browser user = 3 SessionRefs = 3 "devices". A per-session presence map surfaced to the app genuinely conflates "live connection" with "device" and would surface "online on 3 devices" for one laptop. This follows directly from the only granularity the model provides; it is not hypothetical.

CLAIM B (set-me-AWAY ergonomics) is the load-bearing, sharper part and is also real: SessionRef carries nodeId, so a user's sessions are genuinely distributed across cluster nodes. An app handling one session on node X cannot locally iterate the user's sessions on node Y, so a per-session setPresence primitive cannot express the natural per-user intent "set THIS USER away" without a cluster fan-out the app doesn't have. No setPresenceForUser(userId, status) convenience exists. This is a concrete API gap, not a vague observation.

REFUTATION ATTEMPTS REJECTED: (1) "out-of-scope because no RC3 spec" fails — this IS a design review whose job is to catch implicit load-bearing decisions before the spec locks, exactly the RC1 shard-collapse lesson the project applies; the per-session granularity is the default/natural design inherited from RC2. (2) "already handled" fails — the RC2 design review only flagged that RC3 brings "status flags, not just bindings" (schema churn); it never addressed device!=session granularity or the per-user-setter ergonomics, and the docs already use "device" loosely without resolving it. (3) The finding does NOT claim the aggregate is computed incorrectly — it explicitly concedes the aggregate is correct ("ONLINE — fine"); the flaw is semantic labeling + setter ergonomics, so it cannot be refuted by showing the aggregate works.

SEVERITY: MINOR is correct and confirmed. There is no correctness bug, no data loss, no safety/security impact — the aggregate is correct. The flaw is misleading granularity naming plus a missing per-user convenience, both with a clean fix (introduce a stable deviceId to collapse sessions, or rename to connection-granularity and add setPresenceForUser). It does not rise to MAJOR.

blockers: Crash of the last device emits NO OFFLINE transition event: the dominant real-world presence transition (laptop sleep / wifi loss never sends a graceful WebSocket close) is structurally undeliverable. clearPresence runs only on graceful onSessionRemoved; the dead-node reap path is a bare HDEL/SREM with no read-old/recompute-new Lua and no publish, so watchers are told ONLINE forever. This is the RC1 shard-collapse class: the headline guarantee fails on the majority path.

PresenceRegistry.removeAllForNode has NO hook to join: the dead-node fan-out (invalidateCacheForNode L1050-1072) reaps ONLY roomRegistry; userRegistry.removeAllForNode (RedisUserRegistry L154) is implemented but NEVER invoked (confirmed dead code — only call sites are sessionRegistry @ ClusterNodeManager L509 and roomRegistry @ ClusterMessageSender L1069). The RC2 javadoc claim 'Reconciled via removeAllForNode on dead-node cleanup' (UserRegistry L28) is false-as-written, and RC3 'joins the reconciliation fan-out' targets a hook that has no slot for the parallel SPI. This leaks crashed-node user bindings indefinitely (false-ONLINE silent message loss) and defeats RC3 aggregate correctness.

OnAnyRedisSpiRequired does not consider presence.enable (L89-92 reads only offline.enable): a presence.enable=true + offline.enable=false + all-4-custom-core-SPI deployment gates the Redis client/connection beans OFF, so the default RedisPresenceRegistry fails to autowire @Qualifier('nettyClusterRedisConnection') → NoSuchBeanDefinitionException at context startup. This is the exact bug class OnAnyRedisSpiRequired was created (V1.9.0-RC16) to prevent, already extended once for offline in RC2.

mustFixBeforeSpec.finding: Crash-path OFFLINE event is structurally dropped (bare-HDEL reap emits nothing)
mustFixBeforeSpec.decision: removeAllForNode MUST be transition-aware: per presence hash run a Lua that reads old aggregate from HVALS, HDELs all fields whose key starts with 'nodeId|', recomputes the new aggregate, and RETURNs (userId, old, new) for every hash where old!=new; the reaper node then PUBLISHes one PRESENCE_CHANGE per changed user on the reserved channel. Reaping is leader-elected (reaper.tryClaim @ ClusterNodeManager L499) so exactly one OFFLINE event fires per affected user, no dup.

mustFixBeforeSpec.finding: Presence reap has no slot in the dead-node fan-out; RC2 UserRegistry reap is already missing
mustFixBeforeSpec.decision: Add presenceRegistry.removeAllForNode AND userRegistry.removeAllForNode to the cleanup, placed on the leader-elected, retried-on-failure primary path in ClusterNodeManager.doReconciliation (alongside sessionRegistry.removeAllForNode, which re-queues via .exceptionally L528-533) — NOT the fire-and-forget deadNodeCallback (invalidateCacheForNode L1069-1070) that swallows exceptions to log.debug. The spec MUST name the exact call site and its failure/retry semantics, and MUST fix the latent RC2 gap (userRegistry never reaped) so its javadoc stops lying.

Add a presence clause to matches(): boolean presenceEnabled = parse('server.netty.websocket.cluster.presence.enable','false'); return ... || (presenceEnabled && !hasBean(bf, PresenceRegistry.class)). Add a context test (presence-only + all-4-custom-core-SPI) asserting nettyClusterRedisConnection exists, mirroring the RC2 offline + AllFourSpi test.

mustFixBeforeSpec.finding: Reserved __netty_presence__ topic collides with broadcast keyspace and mis-dispatches via onBroadcastMessage
mustFixBeforeSpec.decision: Presence must NOT ride the broadcast-topic path. (1) Use a DEDICATED listener onPresenceMessage subscribed via broker.subscribe('__netty_presence__', this::onPresenceMessage) from start(), gated on presence.enable, UNCONDITIONAL (not driven by getRegisteredUri) so a node with zero local sessions still receives events for users it watches — do NOT route through onBroadcastMessage (L578-594 hard-wires deserializePayload + localSender.topicMessage). Prefer a control-prefixed reserved name (or a new PRESENCE MessageKind, mirroring RC1 ROOM_BROADCAST). (2) Validate at @MessageMapping registration that no app URI equals the reserved name, fail fast at startup (real enforcement, not a javadoc note).

mustFixBeforeSpec.finding: ClusterSessionHookImpl single offlineEnabled flag does not split into identity/offline/presence
mustFixBeforeSpec.decision: Refactor into independent flags: identityEnabled = resolver!=null && userRegistry!=null; offlineEnabled = identityEnabled && offlineStore!=null; presenceEnabled = identityEnabled && presenceRegistry!=null. Gate resolve+register-with-userId-metadata+bindUser/unbindUser on identityEnabled (so presence-only still binds, L106-128 currently gated on offlineEnabled); drainOnConnect on offlineEnabled; setPresence(ONLINE)/clearPresence+publish-on-transition on presenceEnabled. Add presenceRegistry to the constructor (L74-88) and update the config bean (NettyWebSocketClusterConfigure ~L778-797) to compute all three flags and pass presenceRegistry. Add a presence-only context/unit test asserting bindUser+setPresence fire and drain does not.

mustFixBeforeSpec.finding: getPresence device key 'nodeId|sessionId' leaks topology and gives no stable device identity
mustFixBeforeSpec.decision: ONE-WAY-DOOR API decision: do not key the public map on 'nodeId|sessionId'. Either (a) introduce a stable deviceId via a DeviceIdResolver SPI (parallel to UserIdResolver) and return Map<deviceId,status> with NO nodeId exposed, or (b) if per-physical-device id is out of RC3 scope, DROP the device-level map from the public API and return aggregate + a device/connection COUNT. Internal 'nodeId|sessionId' stays the Redis HASH field for reaping but MUST NOT surface in the SPI return type. Freeze this before the spec.

specNotes.finding: Stale-ONLINE window after crash is unbounded by design (only by reconciliation interval); getPresence over-promises
specNotes.decision: Document the bound explicitly in the getPresence javadoc/spec: stale-ONLINE window <= heartbeatTimeoutMs (10000) + reconciliationIntervalMs (15000) + leader-claim + SCAN time (~25s+ at defaults), and that getPresence reflects LAST-KNOWN state, NOT a liveness probe. State that latency-sensitive consumers must treat getPresence as advisory and rely on the sendToUser delivery-time fallback (fresh lookup + offline-queue fallback, ClusterMessageSender L816-858) for correctness — exactly as RC2 framed the no-cache decision. The reap-emitted OFFLINE event (BLOCKER #1 fix) becomes the authoritative self-heal correction signal.

specNotes.finding: Self-delivery echo on the reserved channel double-fires the local PresenceChangeListener
specNotes.decision: onPresenceMessage MUST drop the envelope when envelope.getOriginNodeId().equals(localNodeId), counted as a presence self-delivery-drop meter (mirror onBroadcastMessage L580 / onRoomMessage L721 / onReliableMessage L1017). DECIDE and STATE the local-listener fire-site: choice (a) — the transition hook fires the local PresenceChangeListener directly and publishes for OTHER nodes only (origin suppresses its own echo), matching the documented local-first/cluster-second principle. Pick (a) explicitly so the impl does not fire in both places.

specNotes.finding: Per-session 'device' granularity makes the AWAY model and aggregate semantically noisy; 'set me away' is awkward per-session
specNotes.decision: Tie to the deviceId fix: if a stable deviceId is introduced, collapse multiple sessions of the same deviceId into one device status (any-ONLINE-wins). If per-session is kept for RC3, rename the public key away from 'device' (e.g. Map<connectionId,status>) and add a setPresenceForUser(userId, status) convenience that fans out across the user's sessions (which span nodes per SessionRef) so the app can express 'set THIS USER away' without iterating sessions it cannot see locally.

synthesis.summary:
VERDICT: fixDesignFirst = TRUE. The RC3 multi-device-presence design is NOT ready for spec. All 9 confirmed findings hold against the live code; I re-verified every load-bearing claim. Three BLOCKERs and three shape/wire/gating MAJORs must be resolved before the spec is written.

The design has one systemic root cause: it asserts presence reuses RC2's reconciliation/reap machinery, but that machinery does not actually exist for the parallel SPI. invalidateCacheForNode (ClusterMessageSender L1050-1072) reaps ONLY roomRegistry (L1069); userRegistry.removeAllForNode (RedisUserRegistry L154) is implemented-but-never-called dead code, and the bare HDEL/SREM reap emits no transition event. So RC3-as-described (a) cannot deliver the OFFLINE event on the dominant crash path (BLOCKER #1), (b) has no fan-out hook to join and inherits a latent RC2 leak (BLOCKER #2), and (c) defeats its own aggregate-correctness goal after any node failure. A third, independent BLOCKER (#3) is a hard context-startup failure: OnAnyRedisSpiRequired (L89-92) does not consider presence.enable, so a presence-only + all-custom-core-SPI deployment gates the Redis connection off and the default RedisPresenceRegistry fails to autowire.

The three MAJORs each change a contract the spec must freeze: the reserved __netty_presence__ topic must NOT ride the broadcast path (needs a dedicated unconditional listener + name-reservation guard, not onBroadcastMessage); ClusterSessionHookImpl's single offlineEnabled flag must split into identity/offline/presence so presence-only actually binds users (today it takes the else branch and never calls bindUser); and the public getPresence map must not freeze the leaky, ephemeral 'nodeId|sessionId' key — a one-way-door API decision requiring either a DeviceIdResolver SPI or dropping the device map for an aggregate+count.

Three MINORs are spec-clarifications (stale-ONLINE window bound + advisory-read contract; onPresenceMessage origin self-suppression + listener fire-site; per-session vs per-device naming + setPresenceForUser convenience). Net: fold the 6 must-fix decisions into the design, then write the spec. The core feature concept (Lua-driven transition detection + reserved-channel fan-out + leader-elected reap) is sound; it is the wiring, gating, and the crash/reap event path that are missing or wrong.
